Sneezing is a complex physiological process that is influenced by a variety of factors. Firstly, it is initiated by the "sneeze gene", which triggers a reaction to a small compound present in our nasal cavity. This signal is then processed by the hippocampus in the brain, which is typically associated with memory and learning functions.

Secondly, sneezing serves several critical biological functions. It acts as a protective mechanism, flushing out old cells from the nasal passage. This process also aids the immune system in protecting against viral infections. The speed of a sneeze is determined by reflexive muscle contractions. Despite the common tendency to suppress sneezes, doing so can potentially cause damage to the nose and sinus. Experts, including Sheena Cruikshank, suggest that to sneeze quietly and safely, one should keep their mouth closed.

Lastly, the sound and manner of a sneeze are not solely determined by biological factors. Cultural factors, psychological influences, and societal norms can also shape how a person sneezes. For example, some societies may encourage loud, unabashed sneezes, while others may promote quieter, more discreet sneezes. In conclusion, sneezing is a fascinating process that blends biology, culture, and psychology, reminding us of the intricate interplay between our bodies and our environments.
